blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلنَّـٰزِعَـٰتِ غَرْقًا (١)
By those [angels] who wrest [the soul] violently, (1)
وَٱلنَّـٰشِطَـٰتِ نَشْطًا (٢)
by those who draw [it] out gently, (2)
وَٱلسَّـٰبِحَـٰتِ سَبْحًا (٣)
by those who swim smoothly, (3)
فَٱلسَّـٰبِقَـٰتِ سَبْقًا (٤)
by those who, racing, take the lead, (4)
فَٱلْمُدَبِّرَٰتِ أَمْرًا (٥)
by those who direct the affairs [of creatures]: (5)
يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ ٱلرَّاجِفَةُ (٦)
the day when the Quaker quakes (6)
تَتْبَعُهَا ٱلرَّادِفَةُ (٧)
and is followed by the Successor, (7)
قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ وَاجِفَةٌ (٨)
hearts will be trembling on that day, (8)
أَبْصَـٰرُهَا خَـٰشِعَةٌ (٩)
bearing a humbled look. (9)
يَقُولُونَ أَءِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِى ٱلْحَافِرَةِ (١٠)
They will say, ‘Are we being returned to our earlier state? (10)
أَءِذَا كُنَّا عِظَـٰمًا نَّخِرَةً (١١)
What, even after we have been decayed bones?!’ (11)
قَالُوا۟ تِلْكَ إِذًا كَرَّةٌ خَاسِرَةٌ (١٢)
They will say, ‘This is, then, a ruinous return!’ (12)
فَإِنَّمَا هِىَ زَجْرَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (١٣)
Yet it will be only a single shout, (13)
فَإِذَا هُم بِٱلسَّاهِرَةِ (١٤)
and behold, they will be awake. (14)
هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ (١٥)
Did you receive the story of Moses, (15)
إِذْ نَادَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ بِٱلْوَادِ ٱلْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى (١٦)
when his Lord called out to him in the holy valley of Tuwa? (16)
ٱذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُۥ طَغَىٰ (١٧)
[And said,] ‘Go to Pharaoh, for indeed he has rebelled, (17)
فَقُلْ هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ (١٨)
and say, ‘‘Would you purify yourself? (18)
وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشَىٰ (١٩)
I will guide you to your Lord, that you may fear [Him]?’’ ’ (19)
فَأَرَىٰهُ ٱلْـَٔايَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (٢٠)
Then he showed him the greatest sign. (20)
فَكَذَّبَ وَعَصَىٰ (٢١)
But he denied, and disobeyed. (21)
ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَىٰ (٢٢)
Then he turned back, walking swiftly, (22)
فَحَشَرَ فَنَادَىٰ (٢٣)
and gathered [the people] and proclaimed, (23)
فَقَالَ أَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٢٤)
saying, ‘I am your exalted lord!’ (24)
فَأَخَذَهُ ٱللَّهُ نَكَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَٱلْأُولَىٰٓ (٢٥)
So Allah seized him with the punishment of this life and the Hereafter. (25)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰٓ (٢٦)
There is indeed a moral in that for someone who fears! (26)
ءَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَمِ ٱلسَّمَآءُ ۚ بَنَىٰهَا (٢٧)
Is your creation more prodigious or that of the heaven He has built? (27)
رَفَعَ سَمْكَهَا فَسَوَّىٰهَا (٢٨)
He raised its vault and fashioned it, (28)
وَأَغْطَشَ لَيْلَهَا وَأَخْرَجَ ضُحَىٰهَا (٢٩)
and darkened its night, and brought forth its forenoon. (29)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ دَحَىٰهَآ (٣٠)
Thereafter He spread out the earth, (30)
أَخْرَجَ مِنْهَا مَآءَهَا وَمَرْعَىٰهَا (٣١)
brought forth from it its water and pastures, (31)
وَٱلْجِبَالَ أَرْسَىٰهَا (٣٢)
setting firm the mountains, (32)
مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعَـٰمِكُمْ (٣٣)
as a [place of] sustenance for you and your livestock. (33)
فَإِذَا جَآءَتِ ٱلطَّآمَّةُ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ (٣٤)
When the Greatest Catastrophe befalls (34)
يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا سَعَىٰ (٣٥)
—the day when man will remember his endeavours (35)
وَبُرِّزَتِ ٱلْجَحِيمُ لِمَن يَرَىٰ (٣٦)
and hell is brought into view for those who can see— (36)
فَأَمَّا مَن طَغَىٰ (٣٧)
as for him who has been rebellious (37)
وَءَاثَرَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (٣٨)
and preferred the life of this world, (38)
فَإِنَّ ٱلْجَحِيمَ هِىَ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ (٣٩)
his refuge will indeed be hell. (39)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِۦ وَنَهَى ٱلنَّفْسَ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ (٤٠)
But as for him who is awed to stand before his Lord and restrains his soul from [following] desires, (40)
فَإِنَّ ٱلْجَنَّةَ هِىَ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰ (٤١)
his refuge will indeed be paradise. (41)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains news emphasized by the occurrence of the Resurrection (البعث) — the raising of the dead from their graves — and the Day of Judgment (القيامة) — the final day of reckoning and reward. It presents an argument for it by way of Divine Governance (التدبير الربوبي) — the systematic management of the affairs of creation by the Lord — which concludes that people on that day will be divided into two groups: the companions of Paradise and the companions of Hellfire. The Surah concludes by referring to their questioning of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the timing of the Hour and the answer to it.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالنَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً وَالنَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً وَالسَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً — By those who strip out violently, by those who draw out gently, by those who float serenely, by those who take the lead outstripping, by those who direct the affairs. (79:1-5). The commentators have differed regarding the interpretation of these five verses with a remarkable divergence, despite their agreement that they are oaths (إقسام). Most of them maintain that the response to the oath (جواب القسم) is omitted, the implied meaning being: "I swear by such and such that you shall surely be resurrected."

Regarding His saying: وَالنَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً (By those who strip out violently), it has been said that they are the angels of death who strip the souls from the bodies; and غَرْقاً (violently) is an emphatic verbal noun (مصدر مؤكد) with the additional letters omitted, meaning "with total immersion" (إغراقاً) and intensity in the stripping.

It has also been said that they are the angels who strip the souls of the disbelievers (كفار) from their bodies with severity. It has been said that it refers to death itself, which strips the souls from the bodies with a profound stripping.

It has been said that they are the stars which "strip" from one horizon to disappear in another—meaning they rise from their points of rising to set in their points of setting. It has been said that they are the bows that are drawn with the arrow—meaning they are extended by pulling their strings with total immersion in the drawing; thus, the oath is by the bows of the mujahideen (المجاهدين) — those who strive in the way of Allah — or by the mujahideen themselves. It has also been said that they are the wild animals that move (تنزع) toward pasture.

Regarding His saying: وَالنَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً (By those who draw out gently), "drawing out" (النشط) refers to pulling, exiting, or extracting with gentleness, ease, and the undoing of a knot. It has been said that they are the angels who extract the souls from the bodies. It has been said that they are specifically the angels who extract the souls of the believers (مؤمنين) from their bodies with gentleness and ease, just as the "strippers" (النازعات) are the angels who strip the souls of the disbelievers from their bodies.

It has been said that they are the angels who "draw out" the souls of the disbelievers from their bodies. It has been said that they are the souls of the believers themselves. It has been said that they are the stars that move and travel from one horizon to another. It has been said that they are the arrows that are released (تنشط) from their bows in battles. It has been said that it is death which draws out and extracts the souls from the bodies. It has been said that they are the wild animals that move from one region to another.

Regarding His saying: وَالسَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً (By those who float serenely), it has been said that they are the angels who seize the souls and then hasten with the soul of the believer to Paradise and with the soul of the disbeliever to the Fire. "Floating" (السبح) refers to speed in movement, as a horse is called "floating" (سابح) when it is swift in its running. It has been said that they are the angels who seize the souls of the believers, drawing them out from the bodies with a gentle draw, then leaving them to rest, like one who floats with something in the water and then casts it. It has been said that they are the angels who descend from the heaven in haste. It has been said that they are the stars that "swim" in their orbits, as the Exalted said: وَكُلٌّ فِي فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ — and each swims in an orbit. (21:33).

It has been said that they are the horses of the warriors that "swim" in their running and hasten. It has been said that they are the fates (المنايا) that "swim" in the souls of animals. It has been said that they are the ships that "swim" in the waters. It has been said they are the clouds, and it has been said they are the creatures of the sea.

Regarding His saying: فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً (By those who take the lead outstripping), it has been said that they are the angels in general because they preceded the son of Adam in goodness, faith, and righteous deeds (الصالحات). It has been said they are the angels of death who outstrip with the soul of the believer to Paradise and with the soul of the disbeliever to the Fire. It has been said they are the angels who seize the soul of the believer and outstrip with it to Paradise. It has been said they are the angels of revelation (الوحي) who outstrip the devils with the revelation to the prophets. It has been said they are the souls of the believers who outstrip toward the angels that seize them, out of longing to meet Allah, the Glorified. It has been said they are the stars that outstrip one another in their course. It has been said they are the horses of the warriors that outstrip one another in war. It has been said they are the fates that outstrip hopes.

Regarding His saying: فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً (By those who direct the affairs), it has been said that they are the angels in general who direct the affairs; this is how most have interpreted it, to the point that some claimed a consensus (اتفاق) among the commentators on it. It has been said they are the four angels who direct the affairs of the world: Gabriel (جبرائيل), Michael (ميكائيل), Azrael (عزرائيل), and Israfil (إسرافيل). Gabriel directs the matter of the winds, the armies, and the revelation; Michael directs the matter of the rain and the plants; Azrael is entrusted with seizing the souls; and Israfil descends with the command to them and is the one who will blow the Trumpet (الصور). It has been said that they are the celestial spheres (الأفلاك) in which the command of Allah occurs, and through which the decree (القضاء) runs in the world.

There is also a view that the oath in the verses is by an omitted possessed noun (مضاف محذوف), the implied meaning being: "By the Lord of those who strip..." etc.

You are well aware that the context (سياق) of these five verses is a single, connected context with similar parts. It does not harmonize with many of these views that necessitate a divergence in the meanings of the things sworn by—such as the "strippers" being the angels who seize the souls of the disbelievers, the "drawers" being wild animals, the "floaters" being ships, the "outstrippers" being the fates that outstrip hopes, and the "directors" being the celestial spheres.

Furthermore, many of these views have no evidence from the perspective of the context, except for the mere suitability of the word according to the language for such usage, whether literal (حقيقة) or metaphorical (مجاز).

Moreover, many of them do not suit the context of the verses of the Surah, which mention the Day of Resurrection and argue for its occurrence, as previously discussed in Surah al-Mursalat regarding the correspondence between the oaths in the speech of the Exalted and their response.

What can be said—and Allah knows best—is that the descriptions sworn by in these verses are applicable to the attributes of the angels in their compliance with the commands issued to them from the Presence of Might (ساحة العزة) concerning the governance (تدبير) of the affairs of this witnessed world, and then their undertaking of that governance by the permission of Allah.

The verses are very similar in context to the opening verses of Surah al-Saffat: وَالصَّافَّاتِ صَفًّا فَالزَّاجِراتِ زَجْراً فَالتَّالِياتِ ذِكْراً — By those [angels] set in ranks, by those who drive away [the clouds] with a [stern] driving, by those who recite the Reminder. (37:1-3); and the opening verses of Surah al-Mursalat: وَالْمُرْسَلاتِ عُرْفاً فَالْعاصِفاتِ عَصْفاً وَالنَّاشِراتِ نَشْراً فَالْفارِقاتِ فَرْقاً فَالْمُلْقِياتِ ذِكْراً — By those [angels] sent forth one after another, by those who move as a [violent] tempest, by those who scatter [the clouds] far and wide, by those who separate [the truth from falsehood] decisively, by those who deliver the Reminder. (77:1-5). These describe the angels in their compliance with the command of Allah, except that they describe the angels of revelation. The verses at the beginning of this Surah describe the angels in general in their governance of the affairs of the world by the permission of Allah.

Furthermore, the most apparent of the attributes mentioned in these five verses in its applicability to the angels is His saying: فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً (By those who direct the affairs). He mentioned "governance" (التدبير) in an absolute sense, not restricted to one thing over another; thus, what is meant is the universal governance in its absolute sense. His saying أَمْراً (an affair) is a specification (تمييز) or an object (مفعول به) for "the directors" (المدبرات). Absolute governance is the function of the angels in general; thus, the "directors" refers to the angels in general.

Since His saying: فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً (By those who direct the affairs) begins with the "fa" of consequence (فاء التفريع), which indicates that the attribute of governance branches out from the attribute of outstripping; and likewise His saying: فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً (By those who take the lead outstripping) is joined with the "fa" of consequence, indicating that outstripping branches out from floating—this indicates the homogeneity of the meanings intended by the three verses: وَالسَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً. Their meaning is that they direct the affair after they have outstripped toward it, and they outstrip toward it after they have "floated"—meaning they hastened toward it upon descending. Thus, the "floaters" and the "outstrippers" are the same "directors" among the angels, considered in terms of their descent toward what they were commanded to govern.

Thus, the three verses are in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ مُعَقِّباتٌ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ يَحْفَظُونَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِ اللهِ — He has guardians, before him and behind him, who guard him by Allah’s command. (13:11), according to the previous clarification of its meaning. The angels descend upon things when the causes (الأسباب) have gathered around them and have contended over them regarding existence or non-existence, survival or cessation, and in their various states. Whatever command Allah has decreed for them and has finalized His decree upon, the angel commanded with it—according to the station (المقام) assigned to him—hastens toward it, outstrips others, and completes the cause that necessitates it; thus, what Allah willed comes to be. Understand this.

Since the three verses are intended to refer to the angels' haste in descending upon what they were commanded of an affair, their outstripping toward it, and their directing it, it becomes necessary to interpret His saying: وَالنَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً وَالنَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً (By those who strip out violently, by those who draw out gently) as their extraction and departure from the station of address (موقف الخطاب) toward what they were commanded. Their "stripping violently" (نزعهم غرقاً) is their commencement of the descent toward the objective with intensity and diligence, and their "drawing out" (نشطهم) is their departure from their station toward it, just as their "floating" (سبحهم) is their haste toward it after departure, which is followed by their outstripping toward it and directing the affair by the permission of Allah.

Thus, the five verses are oaths by the attributes the angels assume when they are commanded to direct an affair from the affairs of this witnessed world, from the moment they begin descending toward it until the completion of the governance.

In them is an indication of the system of the celestial governance (التدبير الملكوتي) — the management of affairs in the realm of the angels — upon the occurrence of events, just as the subsequent verses—meaning His saying: هَلْ أَتاكَ (Has there come to you...) etc.—are an indication of the Divine Governance (التدبير الربوبي) manifest in this world.

In the celestial governance, there is an argument for the Resurrection and the Recompense (الجزاء), just as in the witnessed worldly governance there is an argument for it, as will be explained to you, Allah willing.

This is what reflection (تدبر) on the context of the noble verses yields, and it is supported to some extent by the narrations (الأخبار) that will follow in the upcoming traditional discussion, Allah willing.

(A Discourse on the Angels as Intermediaries in Governance)

The angels are intermediaries (وسائط) between Him, the Exalted, and things, both in the beginning (بدءاً) and the return (عوداً), according to what the noble Quran provides. This means they are causes (أسباب) for events above the material causes in the witnessed world, both before the arrival of death and the transition to the life of the Hereafter, and after it.

As for the return (العود)—meaning the state of the appearance of the signs of death, the seizing of the soul, the conducting of the questioning, the reward and punishment of the grave, the causing of all to die by the blowing of the Trumpet, their being brought to life by it, the Gathering (الحشر), the giving of the Book, the setting of the Scales (الموازين), the Reckoning (الحساب), and the driving toward Paradise and the Fire—their intermediation in these matters is beyond the need for explanation. The verses indicating this are many, and there is no need to cite them; the narrations transmitted regarding this from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the People of the House (أهل البيت) عليه‌السلام are beyond counting.

Likewise is their intermediation in the stage of legislation (التشريع), from descending with the revelation, repelling the devils from interfering in it, strengthening the Prophet, supporting the believers, and purifying them through seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار).

As for their intermediation in directing the affairs in this life (النشأة), it is indicated by what is at the beginning of this Surah in the absolute sense of His saying: وَالنَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً وَالنَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً وَالسَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً, according to the previous explanation.

Likewise is His saying, the Exalted: جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلاً أُولِي أَجْنِحَةٍ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ — the originator of the heavens and the earth, who made the angels [His] messengers, possessing wings, two, three or four. (35:1). This is apparent in its absolute sense—according to its previous interpretation—that they were created and their function is to intermediate between Him, the Exalted, and His creation, and they are sent to execute His command. This is understood from His saying, the Exalted, in describing them: بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ — Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command. (21:26-27), and His saying: يَخافُونَ رَبَّهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ — They fear their Lord [who is] above them, and they do what they are commanded. (16:50). The assignment of wings (الجناح) to them is an indication of that.

Thus, the angels have no occupation except intermediating between Him, the Exalted, and His creation by executing His command among them. This is not by way of coincidence—such that Allah, the Glorified, would conduct an affair through their hands and then conduct a similar one without their intermediation—for there is no variation or failure in the Sunnah (السنة) — the established way or law — of the Exalted: إِنَّ رَبِّي عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — Indeed my Lord is on a straight path. (11:56), and He said: فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللهِ تَبْدِيلاً وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللهِ تَحْوِيلاً — But you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent, and you will never find any revision in Allah’s precedent. (35:43).

Part of this intermediation is that some of them are above others in station (مقاماً), and the higher among them commands the lower with something of the governance. This is, in reality, an intermediation from the followed one between Him, the Exalted, and his follower in conveying the command of Allah, the Exalted—such as the intermediation of the Angel of Death in commanding some of his assistants to seize a particular soul. The Exalted said, narrating from the angels: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ — ‘There is none among us but has a known station. (37:164), and He said: مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ — obeyed there and trustworthy. (81:21), and He said: حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَّ — When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth.’ (34:23).

This which has been mentioned regarding their intermediation between Him, the Exalted, and events—meaning their being causes to which events are attributed—does not contradict the attribution of events to their proximate material causes (الأسباب القريبة المادية). For causality (السببية) is longitudinal (طولية), not horizontal (عرضية); that is, the proximate cause is a cause for the event, and the remote cause is a cause for the cause.

Just as their intermediation and the attribution of events to them does not contradict the attribution of events to Him, the Exalted, and His being the sole cause for all of them, as necessitated by the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — the belief in the absolute oneness of Allah in His governance and lordship over all creation. For causality is longitudinal, as you have heard, not horizontal. The attribution of events to the angels does not add anything more than their attribution to their natural proximate causes. The noble Quran has affirmed the attribution of events to natural events just as it has affirmed their attribution to the angels.

None of the causes has independence (استقلال) over against Him, the Exalted, such that it would be cut off from Him, thereby preventing the attribution of what is attributed to it to Allah, the Glorified—as the pagans (الوثنية) claim regarding His, the Exalted, delegation of the governance of affairs to the archangels. Quranic monotheism (التوحيد) denies independence to everything from every aspect: they possess for themselves neither benefit nor harm, nor death, nor life, nor resurrection.

The likeness of things in their attribution to their ordered causes, proximate and remote, and their ending with Allah, the Glorified—in a remote sense—is like the writing that a person writes with his hand and with the pen. The writing has an attribution to the pen, then to the hand which employed the pen for writing, and to the person who employed the hand and the pen for it. The cause in the true sense of its meaning is the person who is independent in causality, without his causality contradicting the attribution of the writing in some way to the hand and to the pen.

There is also no contradiction between what has preceded—that the function of the angels is intermediation in governance—and what appears from His, the Exalted, speech that some or all of them are constant in His, the Exalted, worship (العبادة), His glorification (التسبيح), and prostration (السجود) to Him, such as His saying: وَمَنْ عِنْدَهُ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَلا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ يُسَبِّحُونَ اللَّيْلَ وَالنَّهارَ لا يَفْتُرُونَ — Those who are with Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they weary. They glorify [Him] night and day, and they do not flag. (21:19-20), and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ — Indeed those who are with your Lord are not too proud to worship Him: they glorify Him and prostrate before Him. (7:206).

This is because it is possible that their worship, prostration, and glorification are the very essence of their work in governance and their compliance with the command issued from the Presence of Might through intermediation, as perhaps hinted at by His saying, the Exalted: وَلِلَّهِ يَسْجُدُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ دابَّةٍ وَالْمَلائِكَةُ وَهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ — To Allah prostrates whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth of animals, and the angels [as well], and they are not arrogant. (16:49).

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الرَّاجِفَةُ تَتْبَعُهَا الرَّادِفَةُ — The day when the Quake quakes, followed by the Succession. (79:6-7). The "Quake" (الراجفة) has been interpreted as the great blast (الصيحة) in which there is vibration and agitation, and the "Succession" (الرادفة) as the one that follows and comes after. Accordingly, the two verses apply to the two blowings of the Trumpet (نفختي الصور) indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرى فَإِذا هُمْ قِيامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ — And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon, except those whom Allah wishes. Then it will be blown another time, and behold, they will be standing, looking on. (39:68).

It has been said: "the Quake" (الراجفة) means that which moves with a violent movement—for "shaking" (الرجف) is used intransitively to mean violent movement, and transitively to mean causing violent movement—and what is intended by it is also the first blowing which moves the earth and the mountains; and by "the Succession" (الرادفة), the second blowing which follows the first.

It has been said: what is meant by "the Quake" is the earth, and by "the Succession" the heavens and the stars which will quake, be agitated, split asunder, and vanish. Both views are not without remoteness, especially the latter.

In any case, what is most suitable to the context is that His saying: يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ (The day when [it] quakes...) etc., is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for the response to the omitted oath, to indicate its magnitude and its reaching the ultimate degree of severity, which is: "you shall surely be resurrected." It has been said that يَوْمَ (the day) is in the accusative case (منصوب) based on the meaning of "hearts on that day will be throbbing" (قلوب يومئذ واجفة) on the day when the Quake quakes; this is not without remoteness.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلُوبٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ واجِفَةٌ أَبْصارُها خاشِعَةٌ — Hearts on that day will be throbbing, their eyes humbled. (79:8-9). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "hearts" (قُلُوبٌ) is for categorization; it is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is "throbbing" (واجِفَةٌ). "Throbbing" (الوجيف) is agitation. "On that day" (يَوْمَئِذٍ) is an adverbial phrase related to "throbbing," and the sentence is an inception (استئناف) explaining the attribute of the Day.

His saying: أَبْصارُها خاشِعَةٌ (their eyes humbled), the pronoun in "their eyes" refers to the hearts. The attribution of eyes and their addition to the hearts is because what is meant by "hearts" in such places—to which the cognitive attributes like knowledge, fear, hope, and the like are added—are the souls (النفوس), as previously indicated.

The attribution of humility (الخشوع) to the eyes, while it is a state of the heart, is only because of the appearance of its effect indicating it in the eyes more strongly than in the other limbs.

His saying, the Exalted: يَقُولُونَ أَإِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ — They say, ‘Are we really to be restored to our former state? (79:10). This is a report and narration of their saying in the world, out of their deeming the Resurrection and the Recompense to be far-fetched, and an indication that those whose hearts will be throbbing and whose eyes will be humbled on the Day of Resurrection are the ones who deny the Resurrection while they are in the world and say such and such.

"The former state" (الحافرة), as has been said, is the beginning and start of a thing. The interrogative is for denial (إنكار) out of deeming it far-fetched. The meaning is: these people say, "Are we really to be returned after death to our first state, which is life?"

It has been said: "the former state" (الحافرة) means the excavated (المحفورة), which is the ground of the grave. The meaning would be: "Are we to be returned from our graves after our death as living beings?" This is as you see.

It has been said: the verse reports their confession of the Resurrection on the Day of Resurrection, and the speech is their speech after being brought to life. The interrogative is for amazement (استغراب), as if when they are resurrected and witness what they witness, they find what they witness to be strange and thus ask about the return to life after death.

This is a good meaning if it did not contradict the apparent context.

His saying, the Exalted: أَإِذا كُنَّا عِظاماً نَخِرَةً — What, when we have become decayed bones?’ (79:11). A repetition of the interrogative to emphasize the deeming of it as far-fetched. If life after death was far-fetched, then with the assumption of the decay of bones and the crumbling of parts, it is even more far-fetched. "Decayed" (النخر) with two short vowels means worn out and crumbled. It is said: the bone decays (نخر العظم ينخر نخرا), so it is "decaying" (ناخر) and "decayed" (نخر).

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا تِلْكَ إِذاً كَرَّةٌ خاسِرَةٌ — They say, ‘That, then, will be a ruinous return!’ (79:12). The demonstration (تلك) refers to the meaning of the return understood from His saying: "Are we really to be restored to our former state?" A "return" (الكرة) is a coming back or a turning back. Describing the return as "ruinous" (خاسرة) is either metaphorical—the loser (خاسر) in reality being its owner—or "ruinous" means possessing loss. The meaning is: they said, "That return—which is the return to life after death—is a return characterized by loss."

This is a statement from them which they uttered in mockery—on the assumption that their saying: "Are we really to be restored..." etc., is what they said in the world. Therefore, the context changed and He said: "They say, ‘That, then...’" etc., after His saying: "They say, ‘Are we really to be restored...’" etc. As for the possibility that it is what they will say upon Resurrection, then it is a statement from them by way of pessimism and regret.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ بِالسَّاهِرَةِ — But it will be only a single shout, and behold, they will be on the surface [of the earth]. (79:13-14). The pronoun "it" (هِيَ) refers to the return; it has been said: to the "Succession" (الرادفة), meaning the second blowing. "Shouting" (الزجر) is driving away with a sound and a cry. The second blowing was expressed as a "shout" (زجرة) because of what it contains of moving them from the state of death to the state of life, and from the belly of the earth to its back. "Behold" (فَإِذا) is for suddenness (فجائية). "The surface" (الساهرة) is the level ground or the land devoid of vegetation.

The two verses are in the position of a response to what their saying "Are we really to be restored..." etc., indicates of deeming the Resurrection far-fetched and difficult. The meaning is: their being brought to life after death and their return is not difficult for Us, for their return—or the "Succession" which is the second blowing—is but a single shout, and behold, they are alive on the face of the earth after they were dead in its belly.

Thus, the two verses are in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَمْرُ السَّاعَةِ إِلَّا كَلَمْحِ الْبَصَرِ أَوْ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ — The matter of the Hour is just like the twinkling of an eye, or even swifter. (16:77).

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسى — Did the story of Moses come to you? (79:15). This verse until the completion of twelve verses is an indication of the summary of the story of Moses, his message to Pharaoh, and Pharaoh's rejection of his call until Allah seized him with the punishment of the Hereafter and the first life.

In it is an admonition and a warning to the polytheists (المشركين) who deny the Resurrection and have employed that to reject the religious call, since there is no meaning to the legislation of religion if there were no Return (المعاد) — the return of souls to their bodies for judgment. In it, furthermore, is a consolation for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the denial of his people, and a threat to them, as supported by the direction of the address in His saying: "Did [it] come to you."

In the story, with all that, is an argument for the occurrence of the Resurrection and the Recompense. For the destruction of Pharaoh and his armies with that terrible destruction is a proof of the truth of the message of Moses from the side of Allah to the people. His message from His side, the Exalted, is not complete except by a Lordship (ربوبية) from Him, the Exalted, over the people—contrary to what the polytheists claim, that there is no Lordship for Him, the Exalted, in relation to the people, and that there are lords besides Him, and that He, the Glorified, is the Lord of lords and nothing else.

In His saying: "Did the story of Moses come to you?" is an interrogative for the purpose of encouraging the listener to hear the story, so that he may be consoled by it and it may be a warning for the deniers through its mention of the punishment and a completion of the argument, as previously mentioned.

This type of interrogative does not contradict the listener's prior knowledge of the story, because the purpose is to direct the listener's attention to the story, not a literal question and seeking of information. It is possible that the verses are the first of what Allah narrates of the story of Moses, or they were preceded by the mention of his story as in Surah al-Muzzammil in summary—which is earlier in revelation than Surah al-Nazi'at—and in Surah al-A'raf, Taha, and others in detail.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ ناداهُ رَبُّهُ بِالْوادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوىً — When his Lord called him in the holy valley of Tuwa. (79:16). An adverbial phrase for the "story," and it was the first of what Allah revealed to him, whereby He invested him with the message. "Tuwa" is the name of the holy valley.

His saying, the Exalted: اذْهَبْ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّهُ طَغى — ‘Go to Pharaoh, for he has indeed rebelled. (79:17). An explanation (تفسير) of the call. It has been said: the speech assumes an omitted "saying," i.e., "saying: Go..." etc., or an omitted explanatory "that" (أن المفسرة), i.e., "that: Go..." etc. In both views, the omission is unnecessary. His saying: "for he has indeed rebelled" is the reason (تعليل) for the command.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقُلْ هَلْ لَكَ إِلى أَنْ تَزَكَّى — And say, ‘‘Would you like to purify yourself? (79:18). The object related to "to" (إلى) is omitted, the implied meaning being: "Do you have an inclination toward purifying yourself?" or something similar. What is meant by "purifying oneself" (التزكي) is being cleansed from the filth of rebellion (الطغيان).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَهْدِيَكَ إِلى رَبِّكَ فَتَخْشى — I will guide you to your Lord, so that you should fear [Him].’’ (79:19). Conjoined to His saying: "purify yourself." What is meant by his guiding him to his Lord—as has been said—is making Him known to him and directing him to the gnosis (معرفة) of Him, the Exalted. From this follows the fear (الخشية) of Him which deters from rebellion and exceeding the bounds of servitude (العبودية). The Exalted said: إِنَّما يَخْشى اللهَ مِنْ عِبادِهِ الْعُلَماءُ — Only those of Allah’s servants who possess knowledge fear Him. (35:28).

If what is meant by "purifying oneself" is being cleansed from rebellion through repentance (التوبة) and returning to Allah, the Exalted, then fear would be consequent upon it; and what is meant by it would be the fear that is inseparable from faith (الإيمان), which calls to obedience and deters from disobedience. If it is being cleansed through obedience and avoiding disobedience, then His saying: "I will guide you to your Lord, so that you should fear [Him]" would be an explanation of what preceded it, and the conjunction would be an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَراهُ الْآيَةَ الْكُبْرى — Then he showed him the greatest sign. (79:20). The "fa" is an elided "fa" (فصيحة), and in the speech is an omission and implication, the original being: "So he came to him and called him, and then he showed him..." etc.

What is meant by "the greatest sign" (الآية الكبرى), as appears from the detail of the story, is the sign of the staff. It has been said: what is meant by it is the sum of his miracles which he showed to Pharaoh and his assembly, but this is remote.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكَذَّبَ وَعَصى — But he denied and disobeyed. (79:21). That is, he denied Moses, so he rejected his message and called him a sorcerer, and he disobeyed him in what he commanded him, or he disobeyed Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَدْبَرَ يَسْعى — Then he turned back, striving. (79:22). "Turning back" (الإدبار) is turning away, and "striving" (السعي) is diligence and effort; i.e., then Pharaoh turned away, being diligent and exerting effort in nullifying the matter of Moses and opposing him.

His saying, the Exalted: فَحَشَرَ فَنادى — Then he mustered [his people] and proclaimed, (79:23). "Mustering" (الحشر) is gathering people by unsettling them. What is meant by it is his gathering the people from the inhabitants of his kingdom, as indicated by the branching of His saying: "and proclaimed, saying, ‘I am your exalted lord!’" upon it. For he was claiming Lordship for all the inhabitants of his kingdom, not for a specific group among them.

It has been said: what is meant by "mustering" is the gathering of the sorcerers, because of His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي الْمَدائِنِ حاشِرِينَ — Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities. (26:53), and His saying: فَتَوَلَّى فِرْعَوْنُ فَجَمَعَ كَيْدَهُ ثُمَّ أَتى — So Pharaoh withdrew and put together his stratagem, and then he came. (20:60). In this is the point that there is no evidence that what is meant by "mustering" in this verse is the very same as what is meant by "mustering" and "gathering" in those two verses.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقالَ أَنَا رَبُّكُمُ الْأَعْلى — saying, ‘I am your exalted lord!’ (79:24). A claim of Lordship. Its apparent meaning is that he claimed to be higher in Lordship than the other lords that his people, the idolaters (الوثنيون), used to believe in; thus, he preferred himself over the rest of their deities.

Perhaps his intention by this preference—despite his being an idolater who worshipped deities, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted, narrating from his assembly addressing him: أَتَذَرُ مُوسى وَقَوْمَهُ لِيُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ — ‘Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land, and to abandon you and your gods?’ (7:127)—was that he is the nearest of the deities to them, their provisions run through his hand, the affairs of their lives are set right by his command, and their honor and nobility are preserved by his will, while the other deities are not of this description.

It has been said: his intention by what he said was to prefer himself over everyone who manages their affairs; its summary is a claim of kingship, and that he is above the rest of the guardians of the affairs of the kingdom among governors and officials. Thus, it would be in the sense of his saying in what Allah narrated from him when he said: وَنادى فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ — Pharaoh proclaimed among his people, saying, ‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me...’ (43:51).

This is contrary to the apparent meaning of the speech. In what he said was his saying to his assembly: يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ ما عَلِمْتُ لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرِي — ‘O elite! I do not know of any god for you other than myself.’ (28:38), and his saying to Moses: لَئِنِ اتَّخَذْتَ إِلهَاً غَيْرِي لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ الْمَسْجُونِينَ — ‘If you take any god other than me, I will surely make you one of the imprisoned.’ (26:29).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَخَذَهُ اللهُ نَكالَ الْآخِرَةِ وَالْأُولى — So Allah seized him with the punishment of the Hereafter and the first life. (79:25). "Seizing" (الأخذ) is a metaphor for punishing. "Exemplary punishment" (النكال) is the punishment that deters whoever sees it or hears of it from committing the like of it. The punishment of the Hereafter is an exemplary punishment insofar as its nature is to deter whoever hears of it from committing the disobedience that leads to it, just as the punishment of extirpation in the world is an exemplary punishment.

The meaning is: so Allah seized Pharaoh—i.e., He punished him and made an example of him—with the exemplary punishment of the Hereafter and the first life. As for the punishment of the world, it was his drowning and the drowning of his armies; as for the punishment of the Hereafter, it is his punishment after death. Thus, what is meant by "the first" and "the last" is the world and the Hereafter.

It has been said: what is meant by "the last" is his last word, "I am your exalted lord!"; and by "the first," his first word which he said before that, "I do not know of any god for you other than myself." So Allah seized him for these two words and made an example of him for them. This meaning is not without obscurity.

It has been said: what is meant by "the first" is his denial and disobedience mentioned at the beginning of the story, and by "the last," the word "I am your exalted lord!" mentioned at its end. This is like the one before it.

It has been said: "the first" refers to the first of his disobediences and "the last" to the last of them. The meaning is: Allah seized him with the exemplary punishment for the sum of his disobediences. This also is not without obscurity.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِمَنْ يَخْشى — There is indeed a lesson in that for him who fears. (79:26). The demonstration (ذلِكَ) refers to the story of Moses. It is apparent that the object of "fears" (يَخْشى) is forgotten and turned away from. The meaning is that in this story—the story of Moses—is a lesson for whoever possesses fear and whose instinct it is to fear wretchedness and punishment; and man's instinct is that. Thus, in it is a lesson for whoever is a human being of upright primordial nature (الفطرة).

It has been said: the object is omitted, the implied meaning being: "for him who fears Allah." The previous view is more eloquent.

His saying, the Exalted: أَأَنْتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقاً أَمِ السَّماءُ بَناها — Is your creation more difficult, or the sky’s, which He built? (79:27) until His saying: وَلِأَنْعامِكُمْ — and for your livestock. (79:33). A rebuking address to the polytheists who deny the Resurrection and mock it, by way of censure. It contains the response to their deeming the Resurrection far-fetched in their saying: "Are we really to be restored to our former state? What, when we have become decayed bones?"—in that Allah created what is more difficult in creation than you; thus, He is surely powerful over your creation and your being brought forth in the other life (النشأة الأخرى).

It also contains an indication of the argument for the occurrence of the Resurrection, where He mentions the universal worldly governance and its connection to the human world. Its necessary consequence is His Lordship, the Exalted; and the necessary consequence of Lordship is the validity of prophethood and the establishment of obligations (التكاليف); and the necessary consequence of that is the Recompense whose place is the Resurrection and the Gathering. Therefore, He branched the discourse on the Resurrection upon it by His saying: "So when the Great Catastrophe comes..." etc.

So His saying: "Is your creation more difficult, or the sky’s?" is a rebuking interrogative for the purpose of removing their deeming the Resurrection after death far-fetched. The indication of the detail of the creation of the sky by His saying: "which He built" etc., is proof that what is intended by it is the affirmation that the sky is more difficult in creation.

His saying: بَناها (which He built) is an inception and a detailed explanation of the creation of the sky.

His saying: رَفَعَ سَمْكَها فَسوَّاها — He raised its vault and fashioned it. (79:28). That is, He raised its ceiling and what is elevated of it. Its "fashioning" (تسويتها) is the ordering of its parts and its composition by placing each part in its place necessitated by wisdom, as in His saying: فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي — So when I have fashioned him and breathed into him of My spirit. (15:29).

His saying: وَأَغْطَشَ لَيْلَها وَأَخْرَجَ ضُحاها — He darkened its night and brought forth its day. (79:29). That is, He made its night dark and made its day manifest. The origin of the meaning of "forenoon" (الضحى) is the spreading of the sun and the extension of the day; what is intended by it is the day in general, by the evidence of the contrast. The attribution of the night and the forenoon to the sky is because their original cause is celestial, which is the appearance of dark bodies by the rising of celestial lights like the light of the sun and others, and their disappearance by concealment. Night and day are not exclusive to the earth we are on, but rather they encompass the rest of the dark, illuminated bodies.

His saying: وَالْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ دَحاها — And the earth, after that He spread it out. (79:30). That is, He spread it and extended it after He built the sky, raised its vault, fashioned it, darkened its night, and brought forth its day.

It has been said: the meaning is "and the earth, along with that, He spread it out," as in His saying: عُتُلٍّ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ زَنِيمٍ — [who is] moreover ignoble and base. (68:13). A discussion has already preceded regarding what appears from His, the Exalted, speech on the creation of the sky and the earth in the commentary on Surah Al-Sajdah. Some mentioned that "spreading out" (الدحو) means rolling.

His saying: أَخْرَجَ مِنْها ماءَها وَمَرْعاها — He brought forth from it its water and its pastures. (79:31). It has been said: "pasture" (المرعى) is applied to grazing (الرعي), which is fodder, just as it comes as a mim-infinitive (مصدر ميمي), and a noun of time and place. What is intended by bringing forth its water from it is the gushing of springs and the flowing of rivers upon it. Bringing forth the pasture is the growing of plants upon it from which animals and humans feed. It is apparent that what is intended by "pasture" is plants in general from which animals and humans feed, as suggested by His saying: مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِأَنْعامِكُمْ — as a provision for you and for your livestock. (79:33), not what is exclusive to animals as is predominant in its usage.

His saying: وَالْجِبالَ أَرْساها — And the mountains, He anchored them. (79:32). That is, He made them firm on the earth so that it would not sway with you, and He stored in them waters and minerals, as His other speech informs.

His saying: مَتاعاً لَكُمْ وَلِأَنْعامِكُمْ — as a provision for you and for your livestock. (79:33). That is, He created what was mentioned of the sky and the earth and directed what He directed of their affairs so that it might be a provision for you and for your livestock which He has subjected for you, that you may enjoy it in your lives. This creation and governance, in which is your enjoyment, necessitates upon you the gnosis of your Lord, the fear of His station, and gratitude for His blessing. Thus, there is a day in which you will be recompensed for what you did therein—if good, then good; and if evil, then evil. Just as this creation and governance is more difficult than your creation, it is not for you to deem your second creation far-fetched or difficult for Him, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا جاءَتِ الطَّامَّةُ الْكُبْرى — So when the Great Catastrophe comes. (79:34). In al-Majma': "The Catastrophe" (الطامة) is the high and the dominant. It is said: "This is more catastrophic (أطم) than this," i.e., higher than it. "The bird 'catastrophized' (طم) the tree," i.e., it went above it. The calamity (الداهية) that cannot be repelled is called a "catastrophe" (طامة). [End quote]. What is meant by "the Great Catastrophe" is the Resurrection, because it is a calamity that rises above and dominates every terrible calamity. This is the meaning of its being described as "Great," and it has been used absolutely.

The beginning of the sentence with the "fa" of consequence is to indicate that its content—meaning the coming of the Resurrection—is among the necessary consequences of the creation of the sky and the earth and the establishment of the governance running in them that follows from that, as previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ ما سَعى — the day when man will remember what he has striven for. (79:35). An adverbial phrase for the coming of the Great Catastrophe. "Striving" (السعي) is work with diligence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِمَنْ يَرى — and hell is brought into view for him who sees. (79:36). "Bringing into view" (التبريز) is manifestation. The object of "sees" (يَرى) is forgotten and turned away from. What is meant by "him who sees" is whoever has sight with which he sees. The meaning is: and Hell was made manifest by the removal of the cover from it for every possessor of sight, so they witness it with an ocular witnessing (مشاهدة عيان).

Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ — ‘You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed your veil from you, and so your sight is acute today.’ (50:22), except that the verse of Qaf is broader in meaning.

The verse is apparent in that Hell is created before the Day of Resurrection, and it only appears on that day through the removal of the cover from it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى وَآثَرَ الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا فَإِنَّ الْجَحِيمَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى وَأَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَنَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى فَإِنَّ الْجَنَّةَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى — As for him who was rebellious and preferred the life of this world, hell will indeed be [his] abode. But as for him who was afraid of standing before his Lord and forbade the soul from [following] desire, paradise will indeed be [his] abode. (79:37-41). A detailed explanation of the state of the people on that day in their division into two groups, placed in the position of the summary which is the omitted response to "when" (إذا), sufficing with the detail over the summary. The implied meaning is: "So when the Great Catastrophe comes, the people will be divided into two groups: as for him who was rebellious..." etc.

The Exalted has divided the people in the three verses into the people of Hell and the people of Paradise—and He presented the attribute of the people of Hell because the direction of the speech is toward the polytheists. He defined the people of Hell by what He described them with in His saying: "who was rebellious and preferred the life of this world," and He contrasted their definition with the definition of the people of Paradise by His saying: "who was afraid of standing before his Lord and forbade the soul from [following] desire." The way of what He described the two groups with, in any case, is the way of stating a general rule (الضابط).

Since the two groups are contrasting according to their state, what was explained for each of them of the description was a contrast to the description of the other. Describing the people of Paradise with fear of the station of their Lord—and fear (الخوف) is the being affected of the weak and vanquished by the strong and vanquisher, and his humility and submission to him—necessitates that the rebellion of the people of Hell—and rebellion (الطغيان) is exceeding the limit—is their not being affected by the station of their Lord through arrogance and their departure from the attire of servitude. Thus, they do not humble themselves, nor submit, nor conduct themselves according to what He willed for them, nor do they choose what He chose for them of eternal happiness, but rather what their souls desire of the decoration of the life of this world.

Among the necessary consequences of their rebellion is their choosing the life of this world, which is what He described them with after describing them with rebellion, when He said: "and preferred the life of this world."

Since among the necessary consequences of rebellion is the rejection of the Hereafter and the preference for the life of this world—which is following the soul in what it wants and obeying it in what it desires, and opposing Him, the Exalted, in what He wills—there was for what contrasts with rebellion of the description, which is fear, what contrasts with preference and following the desire of the soul, which is the disposition of deterring from inclining toward the earth and forbidding the soul from following desire. This is His saying in describing the people of Paradise after describing them with fear: "and forbade the soul from [following] desire."

He only included in His description the forbidding of desire, rather than the abandonment of following it in action, because man is weak; perhaps ignorance might lead him to disobedience without arrogance, and Allah is all-forgiving. The Exalted said: وَلِلَّهِ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَساؤُا بِما عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَواحِشَ إِلَّا اللَّمَمَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ واسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ — To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, that He may may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best [reward]—those who avoid major sins and indecencies, except [minor] faults. Indeed your Lord is expansive in forgiveness. (53:31-32), and He said: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ وَنُدْخِلْكُمْ مُدْخَلاً كَرِيماً — If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds and admit you to a noble abode. (4:31).

The meaning of the three verses in providing the general rule for the attribute of the people of Hell and the people of Paradise results in the people of Hell being the people of disbelief and transgression (الفسوق), and the people of Paradise being the people of faith and godwariness (التقوى). There are, besides the two groups, other groups among the oppressed (المستضعفين) and those who confessed their sins, having mixed a righteous deed with another that was evil, and others; their matter is with Allah, the Glorified—perhaps forgiveness will encompass them through intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — or otherwise.

So His saying: "As for him who was rebellious... hell will indeed be [his] abode" (هِيَ الْمَأْوى), i.e., it is his abode, assuming the "al" (ال) is a substitute for the pronoun, or the pronoun is omitted, the implied meaning being: "it is the abode for him."

His saying: "But as for him who was afraid of standing before his Lord" (مَقامَ رَبِّهِ) etc. "Station" (المقام) is a noun of place intended for the place in which a body among bodies stands; this is the origin of its meaning, like its being a noun of time and a mim-infinitive. However, perhaps what a thing possesses of attributes and states is considered a place and a residence for the thing by a kind of figurative regard; thus, it is called a "station" like "rank" (المنزلة), as in His saying, the Exalted, regarding testimony: فَآخَرانِ يَقُومانِ مَقامَهُما — then two others shall stand in their place. (5:107); and the saying of Noah عليه‌السلام to his people in what Allah narrated: إِنْ كانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكُمْ مَقامِي وَتَذْكِيرِي بِآياتِ اللهِ — ‘If my stay [with you] and my reminding [you] of Allah’s signs is hard on you...’ (10:71); and the saying of the angels in what Allah narrated: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ — ‘There is none among us but has a known station.’ (37:164).

So His station, the Exalted, attributed to Him insofar as He is Lord, is the attribute of His Lordship with what it necessitates or what His noble attributes—such as knowledge, absolute power, dominance, victory, mercy, wrath, and what is suitable to them—depend upon. He said, giving notice of it: وَلا تَطْغَوْا فِيهِ فَيَحِلَّ عَلَيْكُمْ غَضَبِي وَمَنْ يَحْلِلْ عَلَيْهِ غَضَبِي فَقَدْ هَوى وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى — ‘and do not be rebellious therein, lest My wrath should descend upon you, and he on whom My wrath descends certainly perishes. Indeed I am most forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then is guided.’ (20:81-82), and He said: نَبِّئْ عِبادِي أَنِّي أَنَا الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ وَأَنَّ عَذابِي هُوَ الْعَذابُ الْأَلِيمُ — Inform My servants that I am indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful, and that My punishment is a painful punishment. (15:49-50).

So His station, the Exalted, which His servants fear, is the stage of His Lordship which is the principle for His mercy and His forgiveness for whoever believes and is godwary, and for His painful punishment and His severe penalty for whoever denies and disobeys.

It has been said: what is meant by "the station of his Lord" is his standing before his Lord on the Day of Resurrection when He questions him about his deeds; this is as you see.

It has been said: the meaning of "feared the station of his Lord" is "feared his Lord" by way of insertion (الإقحام), as has been said regarding His saying: "Honor his stay" (أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Faqih, it is narrated that Ali ibn Mahziyar said: I said to Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام: "Regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى وَالنَّهارِ إِذا تَجَلَّى (By the night when it enshrouds, and the day when it brightens) and His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَالنَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى (By the star when it goes down) and what is similar to this?" He said: "Indeed, for Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is to swear by whatever He wills of His creation—but it is not for His creation to swear except by Him."

I say: A narration in al-Kafi has already preceded regarding this meaning, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام in the commentary on the beginning of Surah al-Najm.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Sa'id ibn Mansur and ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Ali regarding His saying: وَالنَّازِعاتِ غَرْقاً. He said: "They are the angels who strip the souls of the disbelievers." وَالنَّاشِطاتِ نَشْطاً: "They are the angels who draw out the souls of the disbelievers—from between the nails and the skin until they extract them." وَالسَّابِحاتِ سَبْحاً: "They are the angels—who float with the souls of the believers between the heaven and the earth." فَالسَّابِقاتِ سَبْقاً: "They are the angels—who outstrip one another with the souls of the believers to Allah." فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً. He said: "They are the angels—who direct the affair of the servants from year to year."

I say: The narration—if authentic—should be taken as mentioning some of the instances (المصاديق), and his saying: "draw out the souls of the disbelievers from between the nails and the skin until they extract them" is a kind of metaphor for the severity of the punishment.

In it, ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that ibn al-Kawwa' asked him about فَالْمُدَبِّراتِ أَمْراً. He said: "The angels who direct the remembrance of the All-Beneficent and His command."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الرَّاجِفَةُ تَتْبَعُهَا الرَّادِفَةُ. He said: "The earth splits with its inhabitants, and the 'Succession' is the shout."

In it, regarding His saying: أَإِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ. He said: "The Quraysh said: 'Are we to return after death?'"

In it, regarding His saying: تِلْكَ إِذاً كَرَّةٌ خاسِرَةٌ. He said: "They said this by way of mockery."

In it, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: أَإِنَّا لَمَرْدُودُونَ فِي الْحافِرَةِ. He says: "In the new creation." As for His saying: فَإِذا هُمْ بِالسَّاهِرَةِ, "the surface" is the earth—they were in the graves, so when they heard the shout, they came out of their graves—and stood level upon the earth.

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain to Dawud al-Raqqi from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَلِمَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ جَنَّتانِ. He said: "Whoever knows that Allah sees him and hears what he says—and knows what he does of good or evil—and that restrains him from ugly deeds—that is the one who feared the station of his Lord and forbade the soul from desire."

I say: The hadith supports what has preceded regarding the meaning of fear of His station, the Exalted.

In it, with its chain from Yahya ibn Aqil, he said: Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام said: "I only fear for you two things: following desire and long hope—as for following desire, it bars from the truth—and as for long hope, it makes one forget the Hereafter."

Ayahs

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْسَىٰهَا (٤٢)
They ask you concerning the Hour, “When will it set in, (42)
فِيمَ أَنتَ مِن ذِكْرَىٰهَآ (٤٣)
considering your frequent mention of it?” (43)
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ مُنتَهَىٰهَآ (٤٤)
Its outcome is with your Lord. (44)
إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرُ مَن يَخْشَىٰهَا (٤٥)
You are only a warner for those who are afraid it. (45)
كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَهَا لَمْ يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحَىٰهَا (٤٦)
The day they see it, it shall be as if they had not stayed [in the world] except for an evening or forenoon. (46)

(Commentary)

The text addresses their questioning regarding the time of the commencement of the Hour (الساعة) — the Day of Resurrection and final judgment — and responds by stating that its knowledge belongs to no one but Allah, as He has reserved it exclusively for Himself.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها (They ask you about the Hour: when will it set in?) [79:42]. It appears that the use of the imperfect verb "they ask you" (يسألونك) implies continuity; for after the polytheists (المشركون) heard the discourse regarding the Resurrection (القيامة), they would repeatedly approach the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and ask him to specify its time for them, being persistent in this regard. Reference to this has been repeated frequently in the Noble Quran.

The word "setting in" (المرسى) is a mim-infinitve (مصدر ميمي) meaning establishment (الإثبات) and confirmation (الإقرار). His saying: أَيَّانَ مُرْساها (when will it set in?) is an explanation of the question. The meaning is: these deniers of the Hour, who mock it, ask you about the Hour: when is its establishment and confirmation? That is, when will the Resurrection (القيامة) commence?

His saying, the Exalted: فِيمَ أَنْتَ مِنْ ذِكْراها (What do you have to do with its mentioning?) [79:43]. This is a rhetorical question of denial (استفهام إنكاري). The phrase "In what" (فِيمَ) consists of a subject and predicate, and "from" (مِنْ) denotes the starting point of an end (ابتداء الغاية). The term "mentioning" (الذكرى) refers to frequent remembrance (كثرة الذكر), and it is more intensive than "remembrance" (الذكر) according to what al-Raghib mentioned.

The meaning is: In what way are you involved in the frequent mentioning of the Hour? That is, what knowledge of its time do you gain from the perspective of its frequent mention and because of it? In other words, you do not know it through frequent mention.

Alternatively, "mentioning" (الذكرى) could mean the presence of the reality of a thing's meaning in the heart. The meaning—based on the rhetorical denial—would be: You possess no knowledge of its reality and what it entails such that you could encompass its time. This is more appropriate than the previous meaning.

It has also been said: The meaning is that its mention is not among the things related to your mission; rather, you were only sent to warn those who fear it.

It has further been said: "In what" (فِيمَ) is a denial of their question, and His saying: أَنْتَ مِنْ ذِكْراها (you are of its mention) is a new sentence providing the reason for the denial of their question. The meaning would be: Why this question? You are indeed a sign of the Hour (ذكرى الساعة) because your mission is connected to it and you are the Seal of the Prophets (خاتم الأنبياء). This amount of knowledge is sufficient for them. This is similar to what was narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "I and the Hour were sent like these two," and he nearly let one precede the other.

It has also been said: The verse is part of the polytheists' question addressed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, meaning: What knowledge do you have of it and its time? Or: What has come to you while you frequently mention it?

However, as you are aware, the context (السياق) does not harmonize well with any of these latter meanings, and most of them are not free from affectation (تكلف).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلى رَبِّكَ مُنْتَهاها (Its ultimate knowledge rests with your Lord) [79:44]. This serves as the rationale for His saying: فِيمَ أَنْتَ مِنْ ذِكْراها (What do you have to do with its mentioning?). The meaning is: You do not know its time because its ultimate end (منتهاها) is with your Lord; thus, no one knows its reality, its attributes—including the specification of its time—except your Lord. Therefore, they have no right to ask about its time, and it is not within your power to answer them.

It is not far-fetched—and Allah knows best—that the verse provides a rationale in another sense: that the Hour commences with the annihilation of things, the collapse of causes (الأسباب), and the manifestation that there is no sovereignty except for Allah, the One, the All-Paramount (الواحد القهار). Thus, that Day is not attributed to anyone but Him, the Exalted, without any assumed cause—including time (الزمان)—truly mediating between Him and that Day. Therefore, that Day does not truly accept temporal specification (توقيت).

For this reason, no delimitation (تحديد) has appeared in His speech except for delimiting the Day by the termination of the worldly realm (نشأة الدنيا), such as His saying: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ (And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon) [39:68], and similar verses indicating the destruction of the world through the changing of the earth and the heavens, the scattering of the stars, and so forth.

Otherwise, it is delimited by a type of representation (تمثيل) and comparison (تشبيه), such as His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَها لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحاها (The day they see it, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] except for an evening or its forenoon) [79:46], and His saying: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ (The day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] except for an hour of a day) [46:35], and His saying: وَيَوْمَ تَقُومُ السَّاعَةُ يُقْسِمُ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ما لَبِثُوا غَيْرَ ساعَةٍ (And the day the Hour sets in, the guilty will swear that they had not tarried [in the world] but for an hour) [30:55]. Then He mentioned the truth of the matter, saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَالْإِيمانَ لَقَدْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي كِتابِ اللهِ إِلى يَوْمِ الْبَعْثِ فَهذا يَوْمُ الْبَعْثِ (But those who were given knowledge and faith will say, ‘Certainly you have tarried in Allah’s Book until the Day of Resurrection. So this is the Day of Resurrection’) [30:56].

What has been mentioned is alluded to by the fact that in several places in His speech, the Hour only comes suddenly. The Exalted said: ثَقُلَتْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لا تَأْتِيكُمْ إِلَّا بَغْتَةً يَسْئَلُونَكَ كَأَنَّكَ حَفِيٌّ عَنْها قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (It weighs heavy on the heavens and the earth. It will not overtake you but all of a sudden.’ They ask you as if you were in the know of it. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah, but most people do not know.’) [7:187], among other verses.

This is a profound perspective that requires thorough reflection (تدبر) for its completion, so that any perceived contradiction with the apparent meanings (ظواهر) of several verses regarding the Resurrection may be resolved. You should reflect upon His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ (You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed from you your veil, and so your sight is acute today) [50:22], and similar verses. And Allah is the One whose help is sought.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما أَنْتَ مُنْذِرُ مَنْ يَخْشاها (You are only a warner for those who fear it) [79:45]. That is, We have only tasked you with warning those who fear the Hour, not with informing them of the time of its commencement, such that you would answer them regarding its time when they ask you. Thus, the restriction (القصر) in the verse is a restriction of isolation (قصر إفراد), restricting the role of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to warning and negating from him the knowledge of the time and its specification for whoever asks about it. What is meant by "fear" (الخشية), as suits the context, is the fear of it when one is reminded of it—that is, the potentiality of fear (شأنية الخشية), not its actualization (فعليتها) before the warning.

His saying, the Exalted: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَها لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحاها (The day they see it, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] except for an evening or its forenoon) [79:46]. This is an explanation of the proximity of the Hour by way of representation (تمثيل) and comparison (تشبيه); the proximity of the Hour to their worldly life is such that their state when they see it is like the state of those who tarried after their life on earth for an evening (عشية) or the forenoon (ضحى) of that evening—meaning a time whose ratio to a single day is like the ratio of the evening to what preceded it, or the ratio of the forenoon to what preceded it.

It has become clear from what preceded that what is meant by "tarrying" (اللبث) is the stay between the worldly life and the Resurrection (البعث)—that is, their stay in the graves—because the reckoning (الحساب) encompasses the entirety of the worldly life.

It has been said: What is meant is the stay between the time of their questioning about its time and the Resurrection. However, the objection to this is that they only witness their stay in this manner at the time of Resurrection, and the Resurrection—which is the giving of life after death—is only relative to the death that preceded it, not the sum of death and the portion of life between the time of questioning and the time of death.

Furthermore, this does not harmonize with the apparent meanings of other verses addressing their stay before the Resurrection, such as His saying, the Exalted: قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ (He will say, ‘How many years did you tarry on earth?’) [23:112].

It has also been said: What is meant by "tarrying" is the stay in the world, but this is a weak (سخيف) opinion.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding وَأَمَّا مَنْ خافَ مَقامَ رَبِّهِ وَنَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوى ـ فَإِنَّ الْجَنَّةَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى (But as for him who is awed by the standing of his Lord and forbids the soul from [following] desire, his abode will indeed be paradise) [79:40-41], he said: "It refers to the servant who, when confronted with a sin against Allah and having the power to commit it, leaves it out of fear of Allah and the prohibition of Allah, and forbids his soul from it; thus his reward is Paradise." Regarding His saying: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها (They ask you about the Hour: when will it set in?), he said: "When will it commence?" So Allah said: إِلى رَبِّكَ مُنْتَهاها (Its ultimate knowledge rests with your Lord), meaning its knowledge is with Allah. Regarding His saying: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَها لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحاها (The day they see it, it will be as if they had not tarried [in the world] except for an evening or its forenoon), he said: "Part of a day."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim and Ibn Mardawayh narrated with a weak chain from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The polytheists of Mecca asked the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, saying: 'When will the Hour commence?' out of mockery. So the verses يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ أَيَّانَ مُرْساها were revealed."

In the same source, al-Bazzar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Aisha, who said: "The Messenger of Allah continued to be asked about the Hour until Allah revealed to him: فِيمَ أَنْتَ مِنْ ذِكْراها إِلى رَبِّكَ مُنْتَهاها (What do you have to do with its mentioning? Its ultimate knowledge rests with your Lord). After that, he was not asked about it."

I say: It was also narrated by several authors of the books from Urwah in a disconnected (مرسلاً) form, and narrated by several of them from Shihab bin Tariq from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in a similar fashion. However, the context (السياق) does not harmonize with it being a response to the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله own questioning.

In some narrations: "When the Bedouins came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, they would ask him about the Hour. He would look at the youngest person among them and say: 'If this one lives for a century, your Hour will have commenced for you.'" This was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Mardawayh from Aisha.

This is a type of time-specification (توقيت) from which the station of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is far removed. It was revealed to him in many Quranic chapters, especially the Meccan ones, that the knowledge of the Hour is exclusive to Him, the Exalted—none knows it but He—and he was commanded to answer whoever asked him about its time by negating such knowledge from himself.
